ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general black tea beverage class; no specific lychee-flavoured or less-sugar variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR covers sweetened ready-to-drink black tea; no lychee-flavoured or reduced-sugar sub-code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for lychee-flavoured black tea beverage identified in published SNOMED CT release.

preparation_method: served chilled or at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard takeaway cup of lychee black tea (less sugar) is approximately 400 ml (400 g), as commonly served at Singaporean bubble tea and speciality tea stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Lychee Black Tea (Less Sugar) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lychee Black Tea (Less Sugar), cr_id D08010067, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sugar-sweetened beverages and black tea — analogous estimate based on sucrose-sweetened tea
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for lychee-flavoured black tea (less sugar). GI of 57 (medium) is estimated by analogy to sucrose-sweetened black tea entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the 50–65 range for sugar-sweetened tea beverages. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 33.04) / 100 = 18.8, based on a 400 g serving containing 33.04 g carbohydrate. Input record GI confidence was 0.31, reflecting the analogous nature of this estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.26 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk regardless of the low sodium content (1 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Despite the 'less sugar' label, this beverage contains 8.26 g sugar per 100 g, exceeding the 5 g/100 g beverage threshold; individuals with hypertension should avoid it and choose plain unsweetened black tea (teh-o kosong) instead, as dietary sugar independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.26 g sugar/100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g); liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial glycaemic excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This 'less sugar' lychee black tea remains categorically unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes because it still delivers 8.26 g of rapidly absorbed sugar per 100 g with virtually no fibre to blunt the glycaemic response; patients should request 0% sugar or substitute with unsweetened black tea.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages, even reduced-sugar variants, is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased visceral adiposity, and higher CVD risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit this beverage due to its 8.26 g sugar per 100 g content, which contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and metabolic risk when consumed regularly; plain unsweetened black tea is a preferable alternative that retains cardioprotective polyphenols without added sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar load (8.26 g/100 g) with no anti-inflammatory components; sugar promotes systemic inflammation relevant to RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This beverage is not suitable for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis as the residual added sugar promotes pro-inflammatory pathways and no positive anti-inflammatory factor (omega-3, polyphenols, whole-food fibre >3 g, or anti-inflammatory spice) is present; plain unsweetened black tea is recommended to retain polyphenol benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lychee Black Tea (Less Sugar) is a popular Singaporean speciality tea beverage consisting of freshly brewed black tea flavoured with lychee syrup and prepared with a reduced amount of sugar compared to the standard version. It is commonly sold at bubble tea shops, hawker stalls, and speciality tea chains across Singapore, typically served chilled or over ice in a 400 ml takeaway cup. The drink has a light amber colour, a delicate floral-fruity lychee aroma, and a mildly sweet taste with the characteristic astringency of black tea. Despite the 'less sugar' designation, the beverage still contains 8.26 g of sugar per 100 g (approximately 33 g per standard 400 ml serving), all of which derives from added lychee syrup and residual sweetener. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.03 g/100 g, and fat content is effectively zero (confirmed zero by formulation). Protein is trace at 0.01 g/100 g. Energy density is 43 kcal per 100 g (approximately 172 kcal per 400 ml serving). Sodium is very low at 1 mg/100 g. From a clinical perspective, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its rapidly absorbed sugar load and absence of fibre. The glycaemic index is estimated at 57 (medium) by analogy to sucrose-sweetened black tea. For health-conscious consumers, requesting 0% sugar or substituting with plain unsweetened black tea (teh-o kosong) is strongly recommended.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010067) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.31 per input) from the University of Sydney GI Database for sucrose-sweetened black tea, which is the closest defensible approved-source analogue.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — this is a water-based tea beverage with no added fat; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation for the same reason. protein_g is trace (0.01 g/100 g), not zero, so no zero-macro justification is required for protein. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific lychee black tea (less sugar) formulation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D08010067 as of 2026. GI value of 57 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose-sweetened black tea from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI testing of this specific product has not been published. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 400 g standard serving: (57 × 33.04) / 100 = 18.8 g. The 'less sugar' designation reflects a reduced but non-zero sugar formulation; this product remains a sugar-sweetened beverage by clinical classification. Input health_suitability conditions (diabetes, hypertension, rheumatoid_arthritis) have been mapped to the Veritas health_context structure with updated condition keys and clinical guidance.
